channel.title: JLT Daily Dispatch — LinkedIn
channel.description: The JLT Daily Dispatch — LinkedIn Business edition

item.title: Consumer confidence in travel is up. The booking window has narrowed.
item.description:
Travel Weekly's exclusive consumer tracking data shows UK confidence in booking an overseas summer holiday has rallied significantly since late April 2026. The booking window, however, has narrowed.
Three things worth noting for agents this fortnight:
1. Intent is up. The proportion of people planning to travel in the next three months has risen.
2. Conversion is lagging. Clients are planning to travel soon and leaving the booking itself until later.
3. The opportunity sits in the gap. Confidence is the harder problem to solve, and that part has already moved. The nudge between intention and confirmation is where a named agent earns the booking.
Full blog on the website link in the first comment
First comment: https://janinelovestravel.co.uk/consumer-confidence-booking-windows-june-2026

item.title: Scotland fans heading to the 2026 FIFA World Cup are running into ESTA problems before they fly.
item.description:
Scotland fans heading to the 2026 FIFA World Cup are running into ESTA problems before they fly, and Travel Weekly is using the story as a live example of the value an agent brings.
Three things worth knowing:
1. Some Tartan Army ESTAs are being delayed or refused, and Scotland's First Minister has stepped in to help.
2. A refused ESTA closes the Visa Waiver door for that trip, pushing the traveller toward a full US visa application that takes weeks.
3. A Scottish agency has been credited with guiding one client through the issue calmly and in time.
This is the quiet, behind-the-scenes work that protects a client's trip long before they reach the airport.
Full blog on the website link in the first comment
First comment: https://janinelovestravel.co.uk/esta-world-cup-agents-june-2026

item.title: UK travel agencies are reporting one of their strongest weeks of trading this year.
item.description:
UK travel agencies are reporting one of their strongest weeks of trading this year, and the picture is worth a proper look.
Three things to know from this week's Travel Weekly reporting:
One, Barrhead Travel has called it their best year-on-year week since March, beating the spring peaks bounce in mid-June.
Two, the late-demand window has opened across the sector, with multiple agencies reporting the same shape of lift.
Three, the clients booking now are ready to book. The dithering is done, the dates are set, and the conversations are closing warmer and faster than earlier in the year.
A good signal for the agency channel heading into peak summer.
Full blog on the website link in the first comment
First comment: https://janinelovestravel.co.uk/travel-agencies-strong-trading-june-2026

item.title: Three things to know about Riyadh Air's launch this week.
item.description:
Three things to know about Riyadh Air's launch this week.
One: flight RX401 operated its first ever commercial service on 10 June 2026, Riyadh to London Heathrow, on a Boeing 787-9 Dreamliner. London is now daily.
Two: Manchester–Riyadh follows on 23 July 2026, three times weekly. Tickets are on sale now for both. The wider network already takes in Jeddah, Cairo, Dubai and Madrid.
Three: the carrier is backed by Saudi Arabia's Public Investment Fund, with a stated ambition of 100+ destinations by 2030. Loyalty programme is called Sfeer.
Useful context for anyone advising clients on Saudi Arabia, the wider Middle East, or new long-haul carrier options.
Full blog on the website link in the first comment

item.title: Three things to know from the Caribbean this morning.
item.description:
Three things to know from the Caribbean this morning.
One, the St Kitts Tourism Authority has renewed its UK travel agent discount programme.
Two, the scheme includes discounted hotel rates at ten participating properties on the island, plus on-island benefits for agents who travel out in person.
Three, this is a renewal rather than a fresh launch, so the programme has a track record behind it and the tourist board is signalling continued investment in the UK trade.
A useful one to know about for agents with Caribbean enquiries on the desk.
Full blog on the website link in the first comment

item.title: New Citizens Advice research shows more than 4 million people in Britain encountered a problem with a package holiday in the past year. Three things worth knowing.
item.description:
New Citizens Advice research shows more than 4 million people in Britain encountered a problem with a package holiday in the past year. Three things worth knowing.
One, 34% of those who booked a package in the last twelve months reported an issue, and the charity now takes around 14 complaints a day.
Two, 42% of the complaints came from all-inclusive trips booked abroad, with quality issues and customer service failures the largest categories.
Three, 1 in 4 affected travellers said the experience left them stressed or upset, and 17% paid extra to get through.
For travel agents, a useful reminder of where professional support earns its keep.
Full blog on the website link in the first comment
First comment: https://janinelovestravel.co.uk/package-holiday-problems-why-an-agent-matters

item.title: Oceania Cruises has put trade product knowledge in the spotlight, and the results are worth flagging.
item.description:
Oceania Cruises has put trade product knowledge in the spotlight, and the results are worth flagging.
Three things to know:
1. The line ran a UK trade competition to drive engagement, and walked away impressed by the depth of product knowledge across agents. Clare Sacco took the headline win.
2. Oceania's sales leadership has pledged to expand its fam trip programme so more agents can experience the ships in person.
3. For agencies selling premium destination-immersive cruise, this signals continued investment in agent expertise.
A useful read-through for anyone selling cruise at the top end of the market.
Full blog on the website link in the first comment
First comment: https://janinelovestravel.co.uk/oceania-cruises-competition-strength-of-knowledge

item.title: Clia UK 2026 wrapped in Southampton this week, with more than 800 delegates in the room.
item.description:
Clia UK 2026 wrapped in Southampton this week, with more than 800 delegates in the room.
Three things worth knowing for UK travel agents selling cruise this summer:
One, senior cruise line leadership identified UK agents as a "great opportunity" to help fill late-booking capacity over the coming months.
Two, the wider picture is global. The Middle East conflict is still affecting international cruise markets, softening demand from some traditional regions.
Three, the UK trade has held up well by contrast, which is why the cruise lines are looking towards British sellers for the late availability.
Worth keeping in mind when you next scan your active cruise enquiries.
Full blog on the website link in the first comment
First comment: https://janinelovestravel.co.uk/cruise-late-capacity-opportunity-uk-agents

item.title: Silversea Cruises has launched a UK trade referral initiative paying travel agents £100 for every new-to-brand client they introduce who then books a Silversea cruise.
item.description:
Silversea Cruises has launched a UK trade referral initiative paying travel agents £100 for every new-to-brand client they introduce who then books a Silversea cruise.
Three things worth knowing:
1. The window is short. It runs from 1 June to 31 July 2026.
2. It is targeted. The £100 is payable only for first-time Silversea clients, not existing guests.
3. The brief is simple. No tiers, no thresholds. One new client, one booking, one referral fee.
Silversea sits at the ultra-luxury end of the cruise market and has been part of the Royal Caribbean Group since 2018. Source: Travel Weekly.
Full blog on the website link in the first comment
First comment: https://janinelovestravel.co.uk/silversea-100-referral-initiative-uk-agents

item.title: Three things to know about Qantas' Project Sunrise this week.
item.description:
Three things to know about Qantas' Project Sunrise this week.
One: the Airbus A350-1000ULR completed its maiden test flight on 2 June from Toulouse, a 3 hour 43 minute sortie with Airbus' flight test team on board.
Two: around 80 hours of flight testing now begins across roughly two months, including certification of a new lighter galley air cooling system that will roll out across all future Qantas A350s.
Three: Qantas has confirmed it will announce the first Project Sunrise route and timing of inaugural services later this month, with Sydney to London and Sydney to New York both in scope.
Full blog on the website link in the first comment
First comment: https://janinelovestravel.co.uk/qantas-project-sunrise-first-flight-london-sydney
